ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKN covers raw lamb meat broadly; no specific lean-only leg sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of lean lamb leg is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a single portion before cooking, yielding roughly 85–90 g cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, leg, whole (shank half and sirloin), separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #17004 / HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw lean lamb leg (NDB 17004) for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient detail. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable for pure protein/fat foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb leg raw lean contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index field set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not-applicable for a zero-carbohydrate food; gi_category is not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (68 mg/100 g) and high potassium (350 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio supportive of blood pressure control.
hypertension.guidance: Lean lamb leg is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades; its naturally low sodium and high potassium content align with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (21.1 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean muscle maintenance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean lamb leg is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes no dietary carbohydrate or sugar, does not raise postprandial blood glucose, and provides high-quality complete protein to support glycaemic stability when paired with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is low (3.2 g/100 g) with saturated fat approximately 1.3 g/100 g; however, cholesterol is 71 mg/100 g and a small amount of trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is present as naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean lamb leg may be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions (up to 2–3 times per week) given its low total and saturated fat content, but individuals with established cardiovascular disease should monitor overall red meat intake and avoid high-fat cooking methods or added saturated fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 precursor) which can amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production in RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including lean lamb leg to occasional consumption, prioritise anti-inflammatory protein sources such as oily fish, and pair any lamb serving with omega-3-rich foods and anti-inflammatory spices.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb leg, raw, lean is the uncooked hind-leg muscle meat of lamb (Ovis aries) with all separable fat removed, leaving only lean red muscle tissue. The cut displays a deep red colour characteristic of myoglobin-rich red meat, with a firm, dense texture and minimal visible marbling once fat is trimmed. Per 100 g it provides 114 kcal, 21.1 g high-quality complete protein containing all essential amino acids, only 3.2 g total fat (of which approximately 1.3 g is saturated), zero carbohydrate, zero sugar, and zero dietary fibre. Micronutrient highlights include iron (2.1 mg, contributing to haem-iron intake for anaemia prevention), zinc (3.5 mg, supporting immune function), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg, critical for neurological health), niacin (5.7 mg), and potassium (350 mg). Sodium is low at 68 mg/100 g. The food is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal when sourced from a certified halal abattoir. It is widely used in Singaporean mamak cuisine, Middle Eastern grilling, South Asian curries, and Western roasting traditions. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management (no glycaemic impact), hypertension (low sodium, high potassium), and weight management (high protein, low calorie). Moderate caution is advised for cardiovascular disease due to cholesterol content and trace ruminant trans fat, and for rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content in red meat. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates.
common_user_queries: How much protein in raw lamb leg?
Is lean lamb good for diabetes?
Calories in raw lean lamb leg per 100g
Can I eat lamb leg with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fatty acid profile and micronutrient detail. Values are consistent between both approved sources. No estimation or analogy required for core macros.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb leg is a pure animal muscle meat containing no digestible carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same rationale. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no plant fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Saturated fat estimated at 1.3 g/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy NDB 17004 (lamb leg, lean only, raw) cross-reference; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. Monounsaturated fat 1.3 g/100 g and polyunsaturated_fat_g 0.2 g/100 g from USDA SR Legacy cross-reference. Trans fat 0.1 g/100 g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (primarily vaccenic acid), sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported for this specific entry in either the HPB or USDA SR Legacy source. B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g populated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17004 as HPB does not report these fields. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g carbohydrate per 100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol; glycemic_index field set to numeric 0 as required by q4 validator for numeric compliance. The input record classified primary_cuisine as mamak; this has been retained in meal_context but cuisine is classified as Generic/International as raw lamb leg is a universal raw ingredient not exclusive to mamak cuisine.
